splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The split affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the term beyond what was imposed in the judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) against raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than they would under § 2255(h) alone, which requires only newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar in § 2244(b)(1) on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures such as mail-in ballot counting deadlines. The outcome affects whether election-rule challenges must wait until after the election or can proceed beforehand, with significant implications for election administration nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury from election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes, giving them standing to challenge such rules before an election.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's speculative claim that election procedures might affect election outcomes is insufficient to establish Article III injury-in-fact for pre-election challenges.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference to agency interpretations in the context of criminal sentencing enhancements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the rule of lenity should prevail over deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity trumps deference. The issue is of heightened importance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference applies even in the criminal sentencing context, allowing deference to anti-defendant readings of ambiguous Guidelines enhancements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means 'and' or 'or,' which determines whether defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief or whether any single criterion suffices. This split leads to dramatically different sentencing outcomes depending on the circuit, with identical criminal histories yielding different mandatory minimum sentences.

positions.label: 'And' means conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' as conjunctive.

positions.label: 'And' means disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and attempt deserves controlling deference when the guideline text itself is silent on inchoate offenses. This split affects career-offender designations and can dramatically change sentencing ranges for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a permissible interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson and Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds the guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition beyond the text, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits, like the Eighth, categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others permit courts to consider them as one factor among many. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to authorize such consideration, but the Seventh Circuit has held the amendment exceeds the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state controlled substance is not listed on the federal drug schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the substance at issue to be controlled under federal law, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies. The Eighth Circuit holds that state offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling, while other circuits require the substance to be federally controlled. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the career offender guideline, so any state drug offense qualifies as a controlled substance offense.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach that limits the definition.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus rather than civil rights actions (Bivens) to challenge conditions of confinement that affect the fact or duration of imprisonment, such as BOP decisions regarding earned time credits or prerelease custody under the First Step Act.
splits.description: Circuits have long been divided on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for prisoner challenges that implicate the duration of confinement versus the conditions of confinement. The Eighth Circuit acknowledges an 'unresolved conflict in the courts of appeals for more than half a century' on the boundary between habeas and civil rights claims, particularly in the context of BOP decisions about First Step Act time credits and prerelease custody.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-related claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions that affect the fact or duration of confinement, including earned time credits, must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus and not through civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights actions may also be available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights actions to challenge conditions and terms of confinement even when the challenge could also be cognizable in habeas, particularly when the prisoner does not seek immediate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order on a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment filed in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, noting the split is lopsided but acknowledging a genuine disagreement.

positions.label: Civil appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case and the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between a Chapter 13 filing and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit holds that such appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits hold that debtors retain post-petition appreciation upon good-faith conversion. This split affects thousands of consumer bankruptcy cases involving appreciating home values.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A) and § 541(a) compels the conclusion that post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) was enacted to protect good-faith converting debtors and that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation remains with the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose sanctions at supervised release revocation proceedings for a punitive purpose, or whether revocation sanctions must be limited to addressing the breach of trust and protecting the public.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit acknowledges an internal split and broader circuit disagreement on whether district courts may explicitly punish defendants for violations of supervised release. Dissenting judges note that Sixth Circuit precedent allows courts to expressly punish at revocation, placing it at 'the extreme of a circuit split,' while other circuits limit revocation sanctions to non-punitive purposes.

positions.label: Punitive purpose is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider punishment as a purpose when imposing sanctions at supervised release revocation proceedings.

positions.label: Punitive purpose is impermissible
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sanctions should address the breach of trust and protect the public but may not be imposed for punitive purposes, as supervised release revocation is not a separate criminal punishment.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of places of public accommodation under Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, including the availability of compensatory damages?
splits.description: Circuits have reached different conclusions regarding the standards and remedies available under Title II of the Civil Rights Act for discrimination claims against hospitality and business establishments. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the disagreement, which impacts hospitality, retail, and other service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Broader remedies and standards
positions.summary: Holds that broader remedies, including compensatory damages, may be available for discrimination claims under Title II or related statutes governing places of public accommodation.

positions.label: More restrictive remedies and standards
positions.summary: Holds that Title II provides more limited remedies and applies more restrictive standards for establishing discrimination claims against public accommodations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it, while others permit it either alone or in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) allowing it has further complicated the split.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by carriers they selected.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are too tenuously connected to the broker's transportation services to fall within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label that the EPA did not require. The question affects thousands of product liability cases involving agricultural chemicals.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims when they are based on duties that parallel rather than add to FIFRA's requirements, consistent with Bates v. Dow Agrosciences.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas-type challenges — such as arguments about errors in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit courts to consider such claims while others hold compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence, including claims that could also be raised in habeas, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis.

positions.label: May not consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release is not a substitute for habeas corpus and courts may not rely on challenges to the validity of a conviction or sentence as extraordinary and compelling reasons for release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
